Name: curvature-aware_precision_routing
Title: Curvature-Aware Precision Routing: Dynamic Precision Allocation via Hessian Geometry
Short Hypothesis: Input-dependent curvature metrics (e.g., Hessian eigenvalues) provide a precise proxy for quantization sensitivity, enabling dynamic precision allocation that adapts to optimization landscape variations under natural distribution shifts.
Related Work: Existing work, such as Spectral Quantization Routing (founder_1), leverages activation covariance geometry for precision allocation but does not explicitly consider input-dependent curvature diagnostics. Our proposal distinguishes itself by focusing on Hessian-based curvature metrics, which capture optimization landscape variations more directly and enable adaptive precision switching under dynamic input distributions.
Abstract: Mixed-precision inference promises significant computational savings but often relies on static profiling or heuristic calibration, failing to adapt to dynamic input distributions. We propose Curvature-Aware Precision Routing (CAPR), a training-free framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on input-dependent curvature metrics. By computing Hessian eigenvalues and condition numbers on-the-fly using randomized numerical linear algebra, we map these diagnostics to a precision-switching policy that enforces strict latency budgets. We theoretically ground CAPR with PAC-Bayes bounds linking curvature sensitivity to generalization stability under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, CAPR achieves superior throughput-accuracy trade-offs compared to static and covariance-based mixed-precision baselines. By replacing heuristic precision allocation with formalized curvature-aware routing, CAPR provides a mathematically transparent pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.
Experiments: Validate CAPR on WILDS benchmarks, comparing throughput-accuracy trade-offs against static mixed-precision and Spectral Quantization Routing baselines. Metrics: accuracy, latency, memory usage.
Analyze Hessian eigenvalue distributions across layers and inputs to quantify curvature variability. Metrics: eigenvalue decay rates, condition numbers.
Evaluate PAC-Bayes bounds under precision switching, comparing theoretical stability guarantees with empirical generalization gaps. Metrics: PAC-Bayes risk, empirical risk.
Conduct ablation studies to isolate the impact of curvature diagnostics versus covariance-based proxies. Metrics: accuracy drop, latency reduction.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Hessian computation may introduce overhead, though randomized methods mitigate this.
Curvature metrics may exhibit high variability across inputs, complicating precision allocation.
Theoretical bounds may not fully capture empirical behavior under extreme quantization.
Single-GPU validation limits scalability insights for multi-GPU setups.
